data.title: PLTW: Solid Income Play
data.description:
Roundhill PLTR WeeklyPay ETF (PLTW) was launched in March 2025, and outside of its two first weekly distributions, it has been throwing off a high level of income for investors on a consistent basis.

PLTW trades different than its main peer YieldMax PLTR Option Income Strategy ETF (PLTY) in that it owns slightly under 20% of Palantir (PLTR) while using credit swaps to generate income of 120% of Palantir's price movement on a weekly basis, with leverage being a key component of its strategy. PLTY on the other hand is a covered call play.

That means PLTW has the capacity to capture more of the upside price movement of PLTR than PLTY does. Why that's important is there has been growing investor demand for ultra-high-yield ETFs to have more upside potential than the covered calls offer.

Holding some of the underlying and using leverage helps PLTW achieve its income goals with the use of leverage.

The ETF has attracted a significant number of investors, with approximately $241 million in AUM.

The underlying

Palantir has been on a torrid pace over the last couple of years, trading from a little under $15.00 per share to its recent 52-week high of $207.52.

Growing its share price at that pace has predictably resulted in some significant volatility, with it trading at its all-time high on November 3, 2025, and falling back to about $148.00 per share, before rebounding to approximately $166.00 per share as I write.

There of course have been concerns in regard to where Palantir goes from here, not only from the prolonged upward move in its share price, but also the hefty 225.94 forward PE it currently stands at.

While short-term sentiment has been eroding lately, over time I think Palantir should continue to do very well, with the caveat that it has to prove whether or not it's entering into a prolonged period of correction, or it's taking a breath before another long run.

My view at this time is there is no doubt Palantir will continue to move upward over the long haul, but it could struggle for a time before it regains consistent momentum.

Even so, it has had a fairly strong correction, and the worse could be over for now, although it could take another dip before finding its bottom.

As this relates to PLTW, it could go either way in share price in the near term, and whatever happens with PLTR it will, without a doubt, follow suit.

The good news is PLTW has performed very well as far as maintaining its share price, even during the volatility of PLTR, and there's no reason to think it'll perform differently going forward.

That doesn't mean it won't drop further, but it does mean it has the potential to recover nicely while continuing to generate meaningful income to shareholders.

Distributions

As mentioned earlier, PLTW has generated consistent income for as long as it has traded publicly, again, outside of the first two weeks it traded, where distributions were $0.1823 in its first week of trading, and $0.1776 in its second week. Since that time, it hasn't traded below $0.3353 on a weekly basis, which occurred on 11-17-2025.

Distributions for PLTW have been volatile, as they have ranged from the aforementioned $0.1776 to has high as $1.58 per unit per week. The volatility of PLTR is a major reason for that.

While paying out TTM distributions of 66.65% while at the same time finding NAV and/or share price support, it points to the success of its model to this point.

It has endured a number of significant corrections since its inception, and so far has held up well. We have yet to see how it'll respond to a steeper drop in PLTR's share price for a prolonged period of time. When that happens, it'll give us the first glimpse into how NAV responds to that and what level the weekly distributions may fall to.

At the same time, if Palantir surprises many analysts and investors to the upside, PLTW will continue to perform very well for those seeking income without significant NAV or price erosion, and better yet, some growth in those areas.

When considering high yielders like PLTW, I don't care too much about growth because the purpose is to take positions in order to boost income. That said, if an ultra-high yielder like PLTW can maintain or slightly grow NAV, it's the best of both worlds for income investors.

The ETF is set up to do just that, and so far, has proven successful in its income strategy and NAV mitigation. Again, we have yet to see a huge correction in the price of PLTR, so how effective PLTW is in income generation while protecting the downside, has yet to be experienced or proven as to the level of mitigation is really in place.

Conclusion

So far, I like what I see with PLTW in performing what it was designed to do. It throws off a high level of distributions while retaining its NAV and share price. Even during the short duration since inception, there haven't been a lot of high-yielding ETFs that have done as well as PLTW on the combined income and growth side of the equation.

Unless Palantir goes through another strong correction before recovering from the recent one, I think PLTW is going to surprise to the upside in income and growth, understanding that growth will be moderate and not as steep as Palantir's.

As mentioned earlier, what remains to be seen is how PLTW responds to a steep and prolonged correction, whenever that may happen. The key there is PLTR may have a lot of juice left in this growth cycle before that happens, and if that's how it plays out, income investors are going to be very happy with the performance of PLTW.

Overall, I like the structure of PLTW, and whatever the market conditions, I think it has a strong, long-term future that will reward investors in it for the long term.

For the most part, I would largely neglect share price movements and focus on the reason for the creation of the ETF, which is to generate income for shareholders. A lot of investors and analysts talk in terms of growth and NAV erosion with ETFs like PLTW, missing the forest for the trees, as ultra-high yielders are built for the primary purpose of producing income and not trying to generate growth as well.

The very nature of a high-yielding ETF is that it's going to not capture a lot of the upside of the price movement of the underlying in order for it to provide shareholders with income. That's its purpose, and that's what should be focused on in regard to total returns.

data.title: Is There Something Wrong With This Rally? No, And These 7 Graphs Prove It
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. This article is part of a weekly series where I bring valuable insights about economics and investments. Honestly, it's very difficult to be pessimistic about the American stock market when you analyze data in a cold and agnostic way.

In this article, I will cover data on corporate results, investment fund data, seasonality, and investor temperament to deal with this challenging market, which is also full of opportunities in my opinion.

Earnings Season Boosts Prices

The 3rd quarter earnings season is approaching its end, and to date, company revenues have grown 8.4%. It is the biggest increase since 2022, and had contributions from all sectors. Stock prices at all-time highs are driven by results, not narratives.

Another piece of information that makes me even more confident in the thesis is represented in the graph below. American stock funds continue to receive investment flows, and the most curious thing is that December tends to be the most promising month in this metric.

About AI Bubble Narratives
